skill.name: ai-image-generation
skill.description: Generate and edit AI images through the inference.sh belt CLI using models such as GPT-Image-2, FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, and Topaz upscaling.
skill.summary: Generate, edit, upscale, and stitch AI images with inference.sh image models.
skill.author: halt-catch-fire

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 44 external command locations, 10 URL locations, and one weak cryptography blocker. Review shows the command findings are mainly Markdown CLI examples and the crypto finding on SKILL.md line 3 is a false positive, but the skill intentionally enables belt CLI commands that call external image services. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, hidden scripts, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External belt CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash(belt *) and provides commands that run inference.sh apps through the belt CLI. This matches the skill purpose, but it can execute external CLI operations and submit user prompts or image references to remote services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and repeated belt commands are direct evidence of external command use. The commands are scoped to belt and appear legitimate for this image generation skill.

medium_findings.title: External Network Service Dependency
medium_findings.description: The skill depends on inference.sh links and belt app calls for image generation, editing, upscaling, and stitching. Users should expect prompts and image URLs to be processed by external services and model providers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains explicit inference.sh URLs and examples that reference remote image URLs. This is privacy-relevant but aligned with the stated image generation functionality.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Is False Positive
low_findings.description: The high static blocker on SKILL.md line 3 appears to match descriptive model text, not cryptographic code. No evidence found of weak hashing, encryption, password handling, or token processing in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found only frontmatter description text at the reported line. There is no executable cryptographic implementation to assess.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Detections Are Mostly Examples
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown inline code and fenced CLI examples. They are not Ruby backtick execution in source code, although several examples are intended for user-directed execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported patterns are visibly Markdown formatting and shell examples in SKILL.md. The residual risk comes from users or agents choosing to run the documented CLI commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed Bash Access for belt CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The allowed-tools declaration grants Bash access for belt commands. This should be reviewed because belt commands can authenticate, browse apps, and invoke remote model services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools line is explicit and directly controls permitted command execution. The scope is limited to belt, which lowers but does not remove operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Image Inputs in CLI Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The editing, upscaling, and stitching examples use HTTPS image inputs. These examples can send image URLs or image-derived data to external services during execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly include image URL fields. They are placeholders, but the workflow requires remote processing when real URLs are supplied.

content.user_title: Generate AI Images with Model CLIs
content.value_statement: Creating model-specific image prompts can be slow and inconsistent. This skill gives Codex, Claude, and Claude Code command examples for inference.sh image models.
actual_capabilities: Lists inference.sh image models with app IDs and recommended use cases.
Provides belt CLI examples for text-to-image generation.
Shows image editing, upscaling, and stitching command patterns.
Includes examples for GPT-Image-2, FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, and Reve.
Links to inference.sh documentation and related image skills.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI and an inference.sh account before commands can run.
Uses external services, so prompts and image URLs may leave the local environment.
Does not include local model execution or offline image generation.
Model availability, cost, speed, and quality depend on external providers.

use_cases.title: Create Product Mockups
use_cases.description: Generate studio-style product images for early marketing concepts and visual tests.

use_cases.title: Explore Concept Art
use_cases.description: Compare prompts across image models to create visual directions for creative projects.

use_cases.title: Prepare Social Visuals
use_cases.description: Produce and refine images for posts, campaigns, and presentation drafts.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Simple Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to generate an image of [subject] in [style]. Choose a suitable default model and explain the command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Models
prompt_templates.prompt: Create prompts for [visual goal] using GPT-Image-2 and FLUX. Explain which model is better for quality, speed, and text rendering.

prompt_templates.title: Edit an Existing Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to edit the image at [image URL]. Change [specific element] while preserving [details to keep].

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Step Visual Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a workflow to generate [asset], upscale it, and stitch it with [second asset]. Include model choices, command sequence, and privacy considerations.

output_examples.input: Generate a product photo of a ceramic coffee mug with studio lighting.
output_examples.output: A model recommendation, a belt command plan, and a generated image result from the selected inference.sh app.

output_examples.input: Change the background of an existing product image to a beach at sunset.
output_examples.output: An editing workflow that uses the supplied image URL and a prompt focused on background replacement.

output_examples.input: Upscale a draft image for a presentation slide.
output_examples.output: An upscaling command plan using the Topaz image upscaler app and notes about expected external processing.

best_practices: Review each command before execution, especially when image URLs or account actions are involved.
Use non-sensitive prompts and images unless external processing is acceptable.
Choose models based on the task, such as text rendering, editing, speed, or upscaling.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential images or private customer data without approval.
Do not run login or install commands without understanding the target service.
Do not assume every listed model has the same cost, quality, or availability.

faq.question: Does this skill generate images locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh belt CLI to run remote image generation apps.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it support image editing?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for editing images with GPT-Image-2 and related apps.

faq.question: Can it upscale images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents a Topaz image upscaler app through the belt CLI.

faq.question: What accounts or tools are required?
faq.answer: Users need the belt CLI and access to inference.sh services before commands can complete.

faq.question: What is the main security consideration?
faq.answer: Commands can send prompts and image URLs to external services, so users should avoid sensitive inputs.
